full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding a valid research paper meeting all Part A criteria
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: hey so i need to submit a research paper that basically clears all these checklist and also i can't choose from already chosen papers... so get me a paper n verify all the things n also tell what i have to do verify each parameter in the doc ohk? the midsem part a
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Perplexity read both the MidSem_PartA.pdf and the form responses sheet listing already-chosen papers, then suggested a paper and verified it against all 10 mandatory criteria. This gave me a structured understanding of what qualifies.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I cross-checked the 10 criteria manually against the MidSem_PartA.pdf: venue must be CORE A* (2009-2012), year 2009-2012, method must be SVM/GMM/ARIMA variant, contribution must be methodological, dataset must be public, compute must be CPU-only, scope must be student-level, and no restricted data.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Filtering papers by page count to find a short, manageable paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: i want a paper not more than 8-10 pages please
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: The earlier suggestion was 62 pages. Perplexity re-searched and found shorter ICML/KDD conference papers which are typically 8-9 pages by format. This was critical since a shorter paper is more manageable to understand deeply for Part C.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I confirmed that ICML and KDD papers follow strict page limits. Conference papers are shorter than journal papers like JMLR which can run 60+ pages.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding an implementation-focused paper with minimal heavy mathematics
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: les maths heavy and more of implementation
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Perplexity explained the difference between theory-heavy papers (convergence proofs, optimisation theory) vs application-focused papers (real datasets, clear experiments, reproducible code). It suggested NLP-based SVM papers as less math-heavy options.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified this by skimming abstracts and introduction sections of suggested papers. Application papers clearly list datasets and experimental comparisons without derivation-heavy sections.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding something appropriate for a 3rd year undergraduate student
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: something easier as a 3rd year undergrad please
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Perplexity suggested papers whose core concepts can be explained without deep mathematical background — recommending papers that use concepts already taught in standard ML courses like EM algorithm and linear SVM.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified that as a 3rd year student I know EM, SVM, and basic NLP concepts from coursework. I checked that suggested papers are explainable using only these foundations.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding a paper with available code or easily reimplementable experiments
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: get some other please that is implementable
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Perplexity focused on papers with sklearn-compatible implementations or well-known open datasets. It confirmed several NLP papers use standard sklearn pipelines requiring zero additional setup.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I confirmed sklearn is available and verified that standard NLP datasets are auto-downloadable without manual dataset management.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Exploring SVM-based NLP papers as final candidate category
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: ohk let's go for arima based
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: While exploring ARIMA-based options, Perplexity also surfaced SVM-based NLP papers as strong alternatives. This helped me compare both categories before making a final decision on paper type.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I reviewed both ARIMA and SVM-based NLP options and concluded that an SVM-based Twitter sentiment analysis paper was more manageable and better aligned with my coursework background.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Generating the JSON template based on official exam structure
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Advanced Machine Learning Mid-Semester Examination LLM Usage Disclosure and JSON Submission (Mandatory) [full document pasted to generate JSON template]
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Perplexity generated a JSON template based on the exact structure required by the assignment, covering all mandatory fields: student_metadata, llm_tools_used, full_llm_interaction_log, top_5_prompts, and student_declaration.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified the JSON structure matches exactly what is described in the MidSem_PartA.pdf document and confirmed all mandatory fields are present.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Extracting paper metadata and filling the submission form for the selected paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/W11-0705.pdf choosing this...can you help me please? fill the form
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude fetched and read the PDF, extracted the paper title, authors, conference name, year, and helped identify correct answers for every form field including primary method (SVM), reproducibility, and dataset availability.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified the title, authors, year, and conference name directly on the ACL Anthology page for W11-0705. Confirmed the paper is from ACL 2011 Workshop on Language in Social Media.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Writing a reason for choosing this paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Why did you choose this paper?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude provided multiple draft options for the paper selection justification. I used these as a reference to understand what points were worth highlighting — dataset availability, SVM method, and compute feasibility.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Cross-checked the claims about public dataset availability by reading the Acknowledgments section of the paper directly.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Refining the paper selection reason to sound natural for an undergrad
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: a bit more detailed and better way? easy terms as if a 3rd year undergrad is writing
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude rewrote the justification in simpler and more natural language, connecting the paper's content clearly to why it was chosen — dataset availability, CPU-feasible experiments, and SVM as a well-understood classical ML method.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Read through all options provided and selected the one closest to my own reasoning. Made minor edits to match my own voice before submitting.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding what the GitHub JSON file should contain
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained the purpose of the LLM usage JSON file, what fields it should include, and generated a first draft template covering student metadata, tools used, interaction log, and declaration.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Compared the draft against the official exam document after uploading it to verify all required fields were present.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Generating a properly structured JSON file matching the official exam requirements
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: [Uploaded official exam PDF] get a json please
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: After uploading the official exam document, Claude generated a complete and properly formatted JSON file following the exact prescribed structure.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Reviewed the generated JSON field by field against the official exam PDF to ensure nothing was missing or misformatted.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Updating the JSON to include all interactions for complete honest disclosure
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude updated the interaction log to include all interactions including the JSON generation step itself, ensuring the disclosure is fully complete and honest as required by the exam rules.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Manually reviewed all interactions against my actual conversation history with both Claude and Perplexity to confirm nothing was omitted or misrepresented.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Merging both Perplexity and Claude interaction logs into one complete JSON
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: merge both na the claude and perplexity both
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude merged both the Perplexity and Claude interaction logs into a single unified JSON file, ensuring both tools are properly declared and all interactions from both sessions are honestly disclosed in one place.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Reviewed the merged JSON to confirm all 14 interactions are present, both tools are listed under llm_tools_used, and the structure matches the official exam requirements.

top_5_prompts.prompt: hey so i need to submit a research paper that basically clears all these checklist and also i can't choose from already chosen papers... so get me a paper n verify all the things n also tell what i have to do verify each parameter in the doc ohk? the midsem part a
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the starting point of the entire paper selection process. It helped me understand all 10 mandatory criteria for a valid paper selection and gave me a systematic framework to evaluate any paper against the checklist — venue, year, method, reproducibility, dataset, and compute feasibility.

top_5_prompts.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/W11-0705.pdf choosing this...can you help me please? fill the form
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most impactful Claude interaction. It confirmed the final selected paper meets all criteria — SVM as primary method, publicly available dataset, ACL venue, 2011 publication year, and CPU-feasible experiments. It directly determined my form submission answers.

top_5_prompts.prompt: les maths heavy and more of implementation
top_5_prompts.why_important: This shaped my entire paper search strategy. Understanding the difference between theory-heavy and application-focused papers helped me assess feasibility for Part C where I need to explain everything without tools. Choosing an application paper with clear experiments is safer than a proof-heavy theoretical paper.

top_5_prompts.prompt: something easier as a 3rd year undergrad please
top_5_prompts.why_important: This helped map paper concepts to my existing coursework knowledge. Knowing that the selected paper uses SVM and POS-based feature engineering — both topics covered in my ML and NLP courses — means I can confidently explain the paper in Part C without getting stuck on unfamiliar prerequisites.

top_5_prompts.prompt: get some other please that is implementable
top_5_prompts.why_important: Reproducibility is worth 15 marks in the rubric. This prompt confirmed that the final paper's experiments are reproducible using standard Python libraries (sklearn, NLTK) on a personal laptop without GPUs or restricted datasets — directly satisfying the compute feasibility and dataset availability criteria.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part A.
